question_text: In an all-stock acquisition, what is the primary factor that determines whether the deal is accretive or dilutive?

options.text: The size of the target relative to the acquirer
The relative P/E ratios of the acquirer and target

correct_answer: The relative P/E ratios of the acquirer and target
explanation: In an all-stock deal, if the acquirer's P/E ratio is higher than the target's P/E (inclusive of the premium), the deal will generally be accretive. This is because a high-P/E acquirer is using 'expensive' shares to buy 'cheaper' earnings. Conversely, if the acquirer's P/E is lower than the target's effective P/E (after the premium), the deal is dilutive because the acquirer is trading expensive currency for less earnings per dollar. This is why high-growth, high-P/E companies historically preferred stock deals -- their expensive shares made acquisitions naturally accretive.
